sourceTitle: YouTube for Customer Acquisition: The System That Replaces Paid Ads
postAngle: Before you touch YouTube, answer four business math questions. If the numbers don't work, walk away.
linkedinPost:
Before you record a single YouTube video for your business, answer four questions.

1. What is a customer worth to you?
2. What can you afford to spend to acquire one?
3. How many new customers per month make the channel "obviously worth it"?
4. Does search demand actually exist on YouTube for your problem?

If any answer is unclear, you are not ready for YouTube. And that is fine. Better to know now than six months and $15,000 into a content calendar that was never going to convert.

When all four check out, though, something interesting happens. You stop treating YouTube like a brand awareness play and start building what we call an Acquisition Engine: search intent mapped to video topics, voiceover-led production (zero founder camera time), and every video structured to move a viewer toward a specific next step.

The full blueprint covers nine components, from opportunity mapping to measurement to knowing when YouTube is the wrong channel entirely.

Two of those nine are the ones most businesses skip. They are also the reason most business YouTube channels plateau at "nice views, no leads."

https://sellontube.com/blog/the-youtube-acquisition-engine

sourceTitle: YouTube Search Intent: Why It Beats Blog SEO for SaaS and B2B
postAngle: YouTube is a search engine, not a content platform. Businesses treating it like social media are leaving qualified leads on the table.
linkedinPost:
Right now, someone in your target market is typing a problem into YouTube search.

Not "marketing tips." Not "how to go viral."

Something like "how to reduce nurse overtime costs" or "CRM migration without downtime" or "best inventory app for Shopify."

These are not entertainment searches. These are people with budget, authority, and a problem they need solved this quarter.

Here is what happens when you build a video around that exact query:

It ranks. Not for a day. For months, sometimes years.
It pulls steady traffic from people already researching your category.
It compounds as your library grows, because YouTube's algorithm starts connecting your videos to each other.

A single search-intent video ranking for the right query can outperform an entire ad campaign. And unlike ads, you pay for production once.

Almost no B2B companies are targeting bottom-of-funnel YouTube queries with structured content. They are still posting generic tips and hoping the algorithm notices.

The breakdown covers seven reasons search-intent video outperforms blog SEO for B2B, and why this window has not closed yet.

https://sellontube.com/blog/search-intent-youtube-seo-power

sourceTitle: YouTube Marketing ROI: Formula, Real Case Study Data + Free Calculator
postAngle: A 12-month case study showed YouTube delivered 3.25x more conversions than blogging. The real ROI formula is simpler than you think.
linkedinPost:
We ran a 12-month experiment with a Shopify app client. Same audience, same offers, same landing pages. Two channels running in parallel: YouTube and blog.

YouTube: 1,257 conversions.
Blog: 411.

That is 3.25x more conversions from video. And the gap widened every single month.

By month 12, YouTube was converting at nearly 5x the rate of the blog. The blog stayed flat while YouTube compounded.

The catch: month-one YouTube ROI almost always looks terrible. You spend $2,000 on content and get 3 leads. But by month six, those same videos plus the 20 you have published since are collectively driving 30 to 40 leads per month. Your per-lead cost drops by 90% while your paid ads competitor is still paying the same rate per click.

The real formula is not views or subscribers. It is: (New customers from YouTube x Customer LTV) minus monthly content cost.

We built a free calculator that runs this math for your specific business model, price point, and growth goals. Takes about 60 seconds.

https://sellontube.com/blog/youtube-marketing-roi

sourceTitle: The Compounding Effect: How 4 Videos a Month Can Build a Predictable Pipeline in 6 to 12 Months
postAngle: The Library Effect: why months 1-3 feel like nothing is happening, and months 6-12 feel like a different business entirely.
linkedinPost:
Four YouTube videos a month sounds modest. Here is why the math is not modest at all.

Month 1 to 3: Some videos start ranking. Search impressions grow. A few qualified leads trickle in. It feels slow. It is slow.

Month 3 to 6: Videos you thought were dead start waking up. YouTube begins trusting your channel. Recommendation pathways strengthen. Traffic becomes more consistent.

Month 6 to 12: Multiple videos rank simultaneously. Viewers watch 2 to 3 videos in one session. Older videos lift newer ones. Engagement on your back catalog strengthens every new upload.

This is what we call the Library Effect. Each video is a miniature salesperson that works on Sundays, works while you sleep, and never asks for a raise.

After 12 months at four per month, you have 48 search-intent assets. Even if only 10 rank well, that is 10 inbound funnels running 24/7.

The post breaks down exactly why four is the sweet spot, what each phase looks like in practice, and when the pipeline shifts from "promising" to "predictable."

https://sellontube.com/blog/compounding-effect-four-videos-a-month

sourceTitle: YouTube vs Blog for Shopify Apps: 12-Month Experiment (3.25x More Conversions)
postAngle: Blog impressions kept growing while clicks declined. The Great Decoupling is real, and it changes the acquisition math for SaaS.
linkedinPost:
We noticed something strange in our blog analytics around mid-2024.

Impressions kept growing. Clicks kept declining.

Google was showing our content to more people, but fewer were clicking through. The traffic we did get bounced faster. Conversions flatlined.

We call this The Great Decoupling. And if you are relying on blog content for acquisition, it is probably happening to you too.

The cause is not a mystery: AI tools answer top-of-funnel questions before searchers reach your site. Google's own summaries do the same. The blog playbook that worked in 2020 is leaking at the top.

So we ran a 12-month experiment. Same Shopify app, same audience, same offers. YouTube targeting bottom-of-funnel queries alongside the blog.

By May 2025, YouTube drove 65% of all conversions that month. The blog, which we had invested years into building, contributed 13%.

Three specific insights explain why YouTube won, and one of them is a gap so wide that almost no SaaS company is exploiting it yet.

https://sellontube.com/blog/youtube-vs-blog-shopify-app-case-study
